did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Double Decker Convolutional Neural Network (DDCNN) framework for melanoma classification. It employs CNNs for feature extraction (bottleneck features) from dermoscopic images, fuses them with clinical ABCDEF indicators, and uses classifiers such as gradient-boosting, logistic regression, and perceptron-based multi-layer classifiers to distinguish benign lesions from malignant melanoma using the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Double Decker Convolutional Neural Network' (DDCNN) framework for melanoma classification. It details specific methods including feature fusion with ABCDEF indicators, data augmentation, and hair removal, and reports empirical results (accuracy, specificity, precision, AUC) on the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets, comparing performance against other classifiers. It is not a review or survey.

title: DDCNN‑F: double decker convolutional neural network ’F’ feature fusion as a medical image classification framework
brief_summary: This study proposes the DDCNN-F framework, a Double Decker Convolutional Neural Network designed for melanoma classification using dermoscopic images. The method extracts bottleneck features from a Baseline Separated Channel of difficult-to-classify samples and fuses them with clinical ABCDEF indicators, including a novel 'F' flag feature. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets using classifiers such as gradient boosting, logistic regression, and multi-layer perceptrons. The proposed system achieved an accuracy of 93.75%, specificity of 98.4%, precision of 98.56%, and an AUC of 0.98. These results demonstrate that the DDCNN-F framework outperforms other state-of-the-art techniques in distinguishing benign lesions from malignant melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (malignant vs. benign/non-melanoma). The study aims to distinguish malignant melanoma from benign lesions using the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The paper notes that the dataset contains a larger number of benign samples compared to malignant ones, and the model aims to recognize true negatives effectively.

Mentioned in the context of the 'F' flag feature (blue-white veil) to explain potential misclassification issues where a blue nevus might be incorrectly identified as melanoma due to high percentages of blue-white areas. Not a primary target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three kinds of skin cancer along with BCC and melanoma, but not included as a target class in the DDCNN-F experiments which focus on melanoma vs. benign.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer. Not included as a target class in the DDCNN-F experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant melanoma vs. benign/non-melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it performs 'melanoma classification' and 'binary classification tasks'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign) using classifiers such as gradient boosting, logistic regression, and multi-layer perceptrons on fused features from dermoscopic images and clinical ABCDEF indicators. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset from the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) Collaboration for melanoma detection, accessed via Kaggle.

use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020

use.validation: Used for validation via tenfold cross-validation approach on the training set.

datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset used for evaluating the proposed framework, mentioned alongside ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020.

use.training: Used for evaluating the proposed framework.
use.validation: Used for evaluating the proposed framework.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the proposed framework.

datasets_used.name: Baseline Separated Channel (BSC)
datasets_used.description: A curated subset of difficult-to-classify samples (hairy images) extracted from the primary datasets, processed with hair removal and data augmentation to balance classes.

use.training: Used as input for the second deck of the DDCNN to generate bottleneck features.
use.other: Created by combining difficult-to-classify samples identified by the first deck CNN using intra-class variance score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the model's generalisability to new data during the training phase.
Used as a confidence factor to identify difficult-to-classify samples and determine an error ratio threshold for creating the Baseline Separated Channel (BSC).
Used to quantify the proportion of erroneous samples within specific confidence factor ranges to establish a cut-off threshold (0.999995) for sample selection.
Used to determine the optimal range of intra-class variance scores and the corresponding threshold value for separating difficult samples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparison of results. Performance was evaluated using empirical metrics such as accuracy, specificity, precision, and AUC, and improvements were described in terms of percentage points (e.g., +7.34% specificity) without statistical significance testing.

dataset_details.evaluation_method: Tenfold cross-validation on training set; 30% held-out test set

overall_ddcnn_f_results.notes: Gradient boosting classifier achieved the highest specificity (98.47%) with F flag. Overall specificity improvement of +7.34% noted for the best case with F flag. AUC of ~0.96 reported in ROC analysis context.

ISIC 2019.results: The paper states the system was trained using the ISIC 2019 dataset, but does not report specific quantitative metrics or split details for this dataset separately from ISIC 2020 in the provided text.

PAD-UFES-20.results: The paper states the framework was evaluated on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, but does not report specific quantitative metrics for this dataset in the provided text.

architecture_used.main_model: Double Decker Convolutional Neural Network (DDCNN) with 'F' feature fusion
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; standard CNNs used for feature extraction, but no specific pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) are detailed in the provided text.
architecture_used.input_size: Resized to a fixed size (W x H) with C channels; specific pixel dimensions not provided in text.

deck_one.purpose: Extract bottleneck features and identify difficult-to-classify samples using intra-class variance score

deck_two.input_data: Baseline Separated Channel (BSC) - difficult samples with hair removed and augmented

components: Clinical ABCDEF indicators (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution, Flag)
feature_fusion.description: Fusion of CNN-extracted features with clinical bio-indicators

classification_or_output_head.type: Multiple classifiers applied to fused features

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a fixed size (W x H)
Conversion to pixel array (W, H, C)
Hair removal using black hat filter, thresholding, and inpainting algorithm
Data augmentation including horizontal flipping, random rotation (±25%), and Gaussian noise addition (fixed at 0.05)
Binary dilation and erosion using a 3x3 structuring element
Image masking to isolate lesion area
RGB to HSV color space transformation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the Baseline Separated Channel (BSC) dataset to produce mirror images and increase dataset size.
Applied to each image in the BSC dataset with a range of ±25% (25 degrees) towards the left and right to add variety and diversify the dataset.
Applied to the BSC dataset to include randomness; noise level fixed at 0.05 to prevent significant changes in the image. Gaussian distribution mean and variance left at default settings.

conclusions: The proposed DDCNN-F framework, which fuses bottleneck features from a Double Decker CNN with clinical ABCDEF indicators including a novel 'F' flag, effectively distinguishes benign skin lesions from malignant melanoma.
The inclusion of the novel 'F' flag feature significantly improved classification performance, resulting in a highest specificity improvement of +7.34% across classifiers.
The DDCNN-F framework achieved an accuracy of 93.75%, specificity of 98.4%, precision of 98.56%, and an AUC of 0.98 on the ISIC 2020 dataset, outperforming other state-of-the-art techniques.
Among the tested classifiers, the Gradient Boosting Classifier combined with the 'F' flag feature demonstrated the highest specificity (98.47%) and precision (98.56%).

The framework successfully addresses challenges associated with difficult-to-classify samples, such as hairy images, by utilizing a Baseline Separated Channel (BSC) with hair removal and data augmentation.
The study is limited to binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. benign); future work will address general skin lesion classification using visual attention approaches.
While the model is lightweight and computationally efficient (training in 10 hours), automation of image portions containing hair still requires personal intervention.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (melanoma vs. benign) and does not address multi-class skin lesion diagnosis or staging.

The model struggles with 'over-hairy' images; the hair removal algorithm may cause leakage of pigment features due to high-order inpainting, potentially contributing to the remaining 6.25% error rate.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance improvements are described empirically without significance testing.
Specific quantitative results for the ISIC 2019 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets are not provided in the text, limiting the ability to assess performance on these specific benchmarks.
The paper does not explicitly report the use of pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) for the CNN architectures, despite mentioning transfer learning as a general benefit of CNNs.
The 'F' flag feature (blue-white veil) is noted as a potential source of misclassification for blue nevi, which share similar dermoscopic features with melanoma.

Data leakage risk exists in the Baseline Separated Channel (BSC) creation process, as difficult samples are identified by the first deck CNN and then re-trained on in the second deck, potentially leading to optimistic performance estimates if not strictly separated.
The code is available only upon reasonable request, not via an open-source repository, which may hinder reproducibility.
The dataset contains a class imbalance with more benign samples than malignant ones, which the authors address via augmentation but which remains a inherent bias of the source data.
